senses.definition: In some algae and mosses, a female reproductive organ that produces eggs.
examples: The egg develops inside the amphigonium before fertilization.
In this species, each amphigonium contains a single large egg cell.

senses.definition: Having or producing both male and female reproductive organs.
examples: The amphigonous plant can self‑fertilize under certain conditions.
Amphigonous species are common in some groups of algae.

senses.definition: Sexual reproduction involving the fusion of male and female cells or gametes.
examples: Amphigony allows genetic material from two parents to combine.
The biologist contrasted amphigony with asexual reproduction in the lecture.

senses.definition: Having the nature of a nonsense verse or writing that sounds serious but is meaningless.
examples: The text was amusingly amphigoric, full of grand words but no clear idea.
Critics dismissed his explanation as amphigoric and unhelpful.

senses.definition: Plural of amphigory: pieces of writing, especially poems, that sound impressive but are actually nonsense.
examples: The magazine printed several playful amphigories on its last page.
Their conversation turned into amusing amphigories with made‑up words.

senses.definition: A piece of nonsense writing, especially a poem, that uses fancy language but has no real meaning.
examples: The poem is a delightful amphigory that plays with sound more than sense.
He entertained the children with an improvised amphigory.

senses.definition: A nonsense poem or piece of writing that seems learned or serious but is actually meaningless.
examples: The satirical magazine published an amphigouri mocking academic jargon.
Her speech slipped into amphigouri, full of big words but little content.

senses.definition: Plural of amphigouri: nonsense poems or writings that imitate learned or serious style.
examples: The poet’s early works are playful amphigouris written for friends.
Their online posts are clever amphigouris that parody legal language.

senses.definition: A cell nucleus that contains two sets of chromosomes, usually one from each parent, especially after fertilization.
examples: After fusion of the gametes, an amphikaryon is formed.
The researcher examined changes in the amphikaryon during development.

senses.definition: Having a nucleus with two combined sets of chromosomes, typically after the fusion of two different nuclei.
examples: The amphikaryotic cell shows traits from both parent organisms.
These fungi pass through an amphikaryotic stage during their life cycle.

senses.definition: A statement or argument that can be understood in two opposite ways and so may mislead people.
examples: The contract contains an amphilogism that could cause legal disputes.
His reply was an amphilogism, open to both agreement and denial.

senses.definition: Ambiguous speech or wording that can be taken in two different, often opposing, senses.
examples: The witness’s amphilogy weakened her testimony.
Editors try to remove amphilogy from official documents.

senses.definition: In poetry, a metrical foot with three syllables in the pattern stressed–unstressed–stressed.
examples: The word "runaway" is an amphimacer in English verse.
Classical poets sometimes used an amphimacer to vary the rhythm.

senses.definition: Plural of amphimacer: metrical feet of three syllables with the pattern stressed–unstressed–stressed.
examples: The poet scatters amphimacers through the line to create emphasis.
In this stanza, two amphimacers appear side by side.

senses.definition: Involving or produced by the mixing of male and female gametes in sexual reproduction.
examples: Most vertebrates have an amphimictic mode of reproduction.
The species alternates between amphimictic and parthenogenetic generations.

senses.definition: Relating to or characterized by amphimixis, the mixing of gametes in sexual reproduction.
examples: Amphimictical processes increase genetic variation in a population.
The study focused on amphimictical mechanisms in flowering plants.

senses.definition: In a way that involves the mixing of male and female gametes in sexual reproduction.
examples: These organisms reproduce amphimictically under normal conditions.
The population had been breeding amphimictically for many generations.

senses.definition: Plural of amphimixis: acts or instances of fusion between male and female gametes in sexual reproduction.
examples: Different amphimixes can lead to a wide range of genetic combinations.
The researcher recorded several successful amphimixes in the sample.

senses.definition: The union or mixing of male and female gametes in sexual reproduction.
examples: Amphimixis increases genetic variation in a population.
The textbook contrasts amphimixis with various forms of asexual reproduction.

senses.definition: In psychoanalysis, the combination of anal and genital desires in a person’s sexual development.
examples: Some early analysts wrote about amphimixis in childhood sexuality.
The case study mentions amphimixis as a passing phase.

senses.definition: An early stage embryo (morula) formed after the fusion of gametes in sexual reproduction.
examples: The amphimorula divides rapidly before forming a blastula.
Cells in the amphimorula are still totipotent.

senses.definition: Plural of amphimorula: early-stage embryos formed after sexual fertilization.
examples: Several amphimorulae were observed under the microscope.
The researcher compared amphimorulae from different treatments.

senses.definition: Relating to the islands of the central Pacific region around Micronesia and Polynesia.
examples: The museum houses amphinesian artifacts and tools.
Scholars study amphinesian navigation techniques with great interest.

senses.definition: A person from or connected with the islands of the central Pacific region around Micronesia and Polynesia.
examples: An amphinesian guided them through local customs.
Amphinesians have a long history of sea travel.

senses.definition: A former group or class of simple marine molluscs that included chitons and related forms.
examples: Amphineura were once treated as a separate class of molluscs.
Textbooks now place many amphineura in other modern groups.

senses.definition: Having nerves arranged on both sides of the body in a similar way; used especially of some invertebrates.
examples: The amphineurous nervous system is simple but symmetrical.
Many molluscs display an amphineurous pattern in early development.

senses.definition: A cell nucleus formed from the fusion of two different nuclei, usually during fertilization.
examples: An amphinucleus is created when male and female nuclei join.
The amphinucleus contains genetic material from both parents.

senses.definition: In Greek mythology, a musician and king whose playing was so powerful that stones moved to build the walls of Thebes.
examples: Amphion and his brother Zethus were said to found Thebes.
According to the myth, Amphion’s music made the city walls rise into place.

senses.definition: Relating to or characteristic of Amphion or his legendary music.
examples: The composer aimed for an amphionic power in the final chorus.
Critics praised the amphionic quality of the performance.

senses.definition: Plural of amphioxus: small, fish‑like marine animals used as model organisms in studies of vertebrate evolution.
examples: Amphioxi are often dissected in zoology courses.
Researchers compared genes in amphioxi and vertebrates.

senses.definition: A former family name used for lancelets, small fish‑like marine chordates.
examples: Older texts classify these animals in Amphioxidae.
Amphioxidae were once considered a separate family of primitive vertebrates.

senses.definition: Larval forms of amphioxus that resemble small, transparent fish‑like organisms.
examples: Amphioxides drift as part of the plankton in coastal waters.
The students observed amphioxides under the microscope.

senses.definition: An alternative family name proposed for amphioxus‑like animals in some taxonomic systems.
examples: Some older classifications use Amphioxididae for lancelets.
The paper mentioned Amphioxididae as a synonym of another family.

senses.definition: Another form of the name amphioxus, a small, fish‑like marine chordate.
examples: The anatomy of amphioxis is important for understanding vertebrate origins.
Students examined a preserved amphioxis in the lab.

senses.definition: A small, fish‑like marine animal with a simple body plan, used as a model for the early evolution of vertebrates.
examples: Amphioxus lives buried in sand in shallow seas.
Scientists study amphioxus to learn how vertebrates evolved.

senses.definition: Plural of amphioxus: small, fish‑like marine animals important in evolutionary studies.
examples: Amphioxuses are common subjects in comparative anatomy classes.
The shore near the lab has many amphioxuses in the sand.

senses.definition: A molecule that has both water‑attracting and water‑repelling parts in its structure.
examples: Each phospholipid is an amphipath with a polar head and a non‑polar tail.
The amphipaths in the solution formed small spheres called micelles.

senses.definition: Having both a water‑attracting part and a water‑repelling part in the same molecule.
examples: Cell membranes are made of amphipathic phospholipids.
The protein has an amphipathic helix that sits at the membrane surface.

senses.definition: A mixture of partly digested proteins formed during the breakdown of proteins by enzymes.
examples: The solution contained amphipeptone after the enzyme acted on the protein.
Amphipeptone can be further broken down into amino acids.

senses.definition: A molecule that has one part that likes water and another part that avoids water, so it tends to stay at surfaces or form structures in water.
examples: Soap molecules are classic amphiphiles.
Each amphiphile in the membrane has a polar head and a non‑polar tail.

senses.definition: Plural of amphiphile: molecules with both water‑loving and water‑avoiding parts that gather at surfaces or form structures in water.
examples: Amphiphiles self‑assemble into bilayers and micelles.
Biological membranes are made largely of natural amphiphiles.

senses.definition: Describing a molecule that has both a water‑loving and a water‑avoiding part, allowing it to interact with water and fats.
examples: Amphiphilic lipids form the basic structure of cell membranes.
The drug was made amphiphilic so it could cross the membrane.

senses.definition: A genus name given to a particular group of fossil insects or related arthropods (used only in scientific classification).
examples: The fossils were assigned to the genus Amphiphithyra.
Amphiphithyra is mentioned briefly in older taxonomic lists.

senses.definition: Having phloem tissue on both the inner and outer sides of the xylem in a vascular bundle.
examples: Some ferns have an amphiphloic siphonostele.
The stem shows an amphiphloic arrangement of tissues.

senses.definition: Describing a vertebra whose body is flat on both its front and back surfaces.
examples: The tail skeleton consists mainly of amphiplatyan vertebrae.
Amphiplatyan centra allow only limited bending.

senses.definition: A genus of diatoms known for having very fine and regular markings on their silica shells.
examples: Amphipleura is often used to test the resolving power of microscopes.
The sample contained several species of Amphipleura.

senses.definition: Having a full set of chromosomes from each of two different species or forms, as in some hybrid plants.
examples: Wheat is an amphiploid derived from several grass species.
Amphiploid lines often show greater vigor than their parents.

senses.definition: A hybrid organism, especially a plant, that has complete chromosome sets from each of two different species or forms.
examples: This amphiploid shows traits of both parent species.
Amphiploids are widely used in crop improvement.

senses.definition: Plural of amphiploidy: instances or types of having full chromosome sets from two different species or forms.
examples: Different amphiploidies can produce distinct patterns of gene expression.
The study examines amphiploidies in several crop plants.

senses.definition: Plural of amphiploid: hybrid organisms with full chromosome sets from each of two different species.
examples: Amphiploids often combine useful traits from both parents.
Breeders created several amphiploids to improve disease resistance.

senses.definition: The condition of having complete chromosome sets from each of two different species or forms in one organism.
examples: Amphiploidy can restore fertility in some hybrids.
The paper discusses the evolutionary role of amphiploidy in plants.

senses.definition: An animal capable of breathing both in water and on land, usually using gills and lungs or other organs.
examples: The mudskipper is an amphipneust that spends time on shore.
Amphipneusts are of interest to evolutionary biologists.

senses.definition: A group or category of animals described as capable of breathing in both water and air.
examples: Some authors once placed certain lungfish in Amphipneusta.
Amphipneusta includes forms that are adapted to life in two environments.
